data.title: Orion Properties: A Lot Of Moving Parts, But Refinancing Risk Is Off The Table (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Back in Nov. 2025, I downgraded Orion Properties (ONL) to a 'strong sell'. The core reason why I did so, was due to the fact that there were potential refinancing risks associated with this deep-value office REIT, that I just didn't think was worth investing in. Especially considering the fact that many (hybrid) office REITs are currently trading at very low valuations, without the refinancing risk associated with it. I'm talking about many of my contrarian REIT plays: AH Realty Trust (AHRT), Piedmont Realty Trust (PDM) and Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN).

I like turnaround REITs that are oversold because of Mr. Market's moods swings. What I don't like though, are unknown risks that are impossible to analyse. That's what I talked about in my Nov. 2025 article, when I said the following:

If Orion Properties fails to refinance its revolver, this could destroy shareholder value. How? By issuing (very) high-interest debt, a share issuance or a distressed asset sale at low prices.

Opportunity costs combined with this major unknown risk to the equity value of the firm, made me opt for a double downgrade.

Today I will take another look at Orion Properties, to see if this REIT deserves a rating upgrade or not.

Let's take a closer look together!

Refinancing risk is dealt with

During the latest Q4 2025 conference call, management said that they've been able to refinance the credit facility revolver, eliminating the 'going concern' that lead me to decide to double downgrade the stock:

on February 18, we entered into a credit agreement for a new senior secured credit facility revolver, which refinances our original credit facility revolver. The new credit facility revolver extends maturity date until February 2029, including two 6-month borrower extension options. It reduces the lender's commitment to $215 million to more closely align with our business plan, reduces the interest rate margin on our borrowings by 50 basis points to SOFR plus 2.75% and eliminates the 10 basis point SOFR adjustment, which will help to lower future interest expense. As of March 5, 2026, we had $127 million outstanding and $88 million of borrowing capacity under our new credit facility revolver.

Next to credit facility, more good news was shared on the refinancing front:

Second, we extended our existing $355 million CMBS loan by 3.5 years to August 2030, inclusive of two extension options totaling 18 months. These very significant achievements give us the financial flexibility and term to continue to execute on our business plan.

It's a good thing that the CMBS loan was extended by 3.5 years, because we don't have to worry about a potentially destructive equity issuance anymore. Especially considering where ONL's stock is trading right now (around $2.50 versus $19 back in 2021).

Me and other authors have written extensively about the fact that ONL carried a lot of vacant properties on the balance sheet. A big part of why the stock is trading at depressed levels relates to the large amount of capital expenditures and leasing costs associated with re-tenanting vacant properties.

During the CC, management expanded on this specific topic as well:

... our focus was on selling properties where we felt the re-leasing prospects did not outweigh the burden of continuing to carry them. These sale transactions will substantially reduce the estimated carry costs associated with these vacant properties by a combined $10.3 million annually.

Repositioning shapes a brighter future

Back in March. 2025, Orion Properties removed 'office' from their name. Many other (office) REITs (PDM, AHRT, BDN) did as well. Management is actively trying to reposition ONL in becoming a future-proof REIT that doesn't rely on (single tenant) office properties that just aren't in high demand (expect the A-tier ones). Management said the following when talking about their portfolio:

We are also actively evaluating opportunities to recycle a modest percentage of these proceeds into acquisitions. As we continue to shift our portfolio concentration away from traditional suburban office properties and toward Dedicated Use Assets or DUAs, where our tenants perform work that cannot be replicated from home or relocated to a generic office setting.

These property types include medical, lab, R&D, flex and government properties, all of which we already own. Our experience is that these assets tend to exhibit stronger renewal trends, higher tenant investment and more durable cash flows.

In the end, you can't invest by just trusting management's promises of a brighter future. That's why it is a good thing that management shared some hard data to show some proof of work:

At year-end, approximately 35.8% of our portfolio by annualized base rent consisted of dedicated use assets versus 31.8% at the end of 2024. And we expect this percentage will continue to increase over time through disposition activity and targeted acquisitions.

Bad news from the Arch Street joint venture

Back in March 2025, I shared my concern regarding ONL's 20% share in a joint venture (JV) with Arch Street (chapter: somewhat concerning JV member loan?). Back than, the fact that ONL provided liquidity infusion to the JV with a high interest tied to it (15%) worried me. Sure, there were good reasons to do so, but a 15% interest rate means elevated risks.

During Q4 2025, we learned that ONL's JV partner was unable to make a $16 million principal prepayment:

Due to the capital constraints of our joint venture partner, the joint venture was unable to make an approximately $16 million loan principal prepayment to satisfy the 60% loan-to-value condition to extend this debt obligation until November 27, 2026.

The lenders have been providing short-term extensions while the joint venture remains in active cooperative discussions with the lenders with respect to the plans of the portfolio and an additional extension. Further, the joint venture has entered into a contract to sell one of the assets out of the portfolio and is in active discussions with the lenders on an additional asset sales to repay debt.

At the end of the CC, management tried to maintain a positive tone regarding the JV. Even though I believe management to be straight-forward and fair in their assessment, ONL really needs expenses (more on this in a bit) to go down and revenue to go up. But I do think that management is right regarding the positive equity embedded in the JV:

While we have written our investment in the JV down due to the uncertainty around the debt financing and our partners' ability to meet capital calls, we continue to believe that the portfolio, which is performing with an occupancy rate of 100% and a weighted average lease term of 6.3 years has positive equity. We expect to continue to work with the JVs lenders and our JV partner to find their way to collect our member loan in full and unlock our equity.

This is just bad news, because ONL now reduced the carrying value of its investment to $0 and recorded a loan loss reserve against a member loan to the Arch Street joint venture. Sure, the impairment has to be done due to accounting rules, but there is financial damage nonetheless. Since the Arch Street JV contributed approx. $0.05 of 2025 core FFO (2025 core FFO came in at $0.78), we are talking about 6.4% of full-year FFO. Quite meaningful if you ask me. Especially given the fact that capital expenditures and leasing costs associated with getting in new tenants and keeping vacant properties up-to-date, are still draining cash. You can see that even though (non-cash) straight-line decreased quite meaningfully. Straight-line rent can be significantly lower for short-duration leases because there is less time to average rent escalations over the lease term. But still, revenue maintaining CapEx is coming down very slowly, leading to a current negative funds available for distribution [FAD] of minus $0.17 for the quarter. This number can change rapidly over time as illustrated by the FAD in Q4 2024 ($0.02) versus Q3 2025 (minus $0.22) shown below, but making an investment case for a REIT that carries a big proportion of less desirable office properties is hard to make when there is such a cash drain going on for multiple quarters in a row.

I will show you that the situation seems to be changing for the better, so it's important to keep an eye out on this performance metric specifically.

With small REITs like ONL (58 operating properties), there is more earnings volatility (or volatility in any performance metric to be honest). But there seem to be signs that the (office) market has reached a bottom. Something that we are hearing across the broader (office) REIT market. Let me share with you one example: You can see that rental rates on new (re)leases are materially higher compared to 2024, with expenses related to tenant incentives trending down. Early signs of normalizing supply-demand dynamics. This makes sense considering the fact that the office market is currently showing negative new supply hitting the market. This basically means that no new supply is coming to the market, but conversions or downright removing inventory does happen.

I've shown the following graph below in a couple of my other articles, but it makes sense to put it out once again to show you how market dynamics are changing for the better:

Vacant property operating expenses increasing?

When I analyzed the Q4 2024 results, I showed the following regarding expenses related to keeping vacant properties up-to-date:

We were talking about $19.31 million during 2024. During 2025, this number crept up to $23.37 million. When excluding all of these cash-draining assets from the balance sheet, the negative FAD number would almost turn positive.

It is important to notice however that when you make a quarter-over-quarter comparison, the picture looks a lot brighter. The grand total of vacant property operating expenses came in at $18.9 million during the first nine months of 2025 (page 19), with total square feet of (temporarily) vacant space decreasing from 2,473 thousand square feet during Q3 2025 to 1,862 square feet during Q4 2025. So we are witnessing dispositions of non-core (vacant) assets that should bring costs down and therefore bringing FAD back towards positive territory once again.

Closing remarks

Orion Properties is a REIT that shows characteristics of a deep-value play. My followers know that I'm always intrigued by these type of REITs. As I've shown in previous articles on ONL, there is embedded value in the company's equity. For those who are interested, just revisit my Q1 2025 equity value calculation based on a 10% cap rate (chapter: A depressed cap rate) to see that the intrinsic value of the company's stock should at least come in around $5.

It's a good thing that refinancing risk is currently off the table and vacant property operating expenses are coming down. But CapEx is still elevated and the dividend is not even close to being covered by free cashflow. The fact that they had to write of their JV investment might be a bit overdone because of accounting rules demanding management to do so, but its still a big headwind when a REIT is trying to improve its cashflow.

All things considered, the unanalyzable refinancing threat has been dealt with, so we can start to truly analyze this REIT again. Trading at a depressed valuations, it is a deep-value turnaround REIT once again.

Management believes 2026 to be the turnaround year:

Leasing momentum remains constructive so far in 2026. Our pipeline is robust, and we have over 1 million square feet in either discussion or documentation stages which includes several full building leases as well as longer duration renewals and new leases with terms materially greater than the average of our portfolio.

2026 core FFO is expected to come in at around $0.69 - $0.76. At the current price of around $2.50, the P/FFO multiple sits at around 3.44x. This looks quite low, but there are just higher quality (office) REITs like Piedmont Realty Trust (5.42x) and Brandywine Realty Trust (5.60x) that are disliked by the market as well, but show better operational performance and have a way better dividend coverage (prospect).

I assign ONL a cautious Hold (once again), because of the embedded value of the equity compared to the trading range of the stock. But I won't recommend investors to make this one a core holding of their portfolio. There are peers that are more attractively priced and have a meaningfully better risk-to-reward profile, so invest accordingly. This could change in the coming quarters, so I will keep Orion Properties on my watchlist.

As always, thanks for reading!

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Is The GLP‑1 Golden Age Already Behind It?
data.description:
Elevator pitch

I see a lot of challenges with Novo Nordisk (NVO) (NOVO:CA) (NONOF):

  1. Novo Nordisk's share in the obesity and diabetes market is eroding
  2. Pricing headwinds are leading to margin deterioration
  3. Patent expirations are another headwind to sales and profits
  4. Undervaluation of NVO stock is an upside risk
  5. I expect the downtrend to continue

Novo Nordisk's share in the obesity and diabetes market is eroding in most markets

Novo Nordisk has steadily been losing market share in its key markets in the broader diabetes medication market and GLP-1 drugs:

You may note in the above charts that China is an exception for the company, as it is gaining share here. However, this is a minuscule portion of overall revenues:

A key reason for this share loss is that Eli Lilly (LLY) has been a formidable contender with its Zepbound and Mounjaro GLP-1 drugs:

The company has ceded U.S. market leadership to Eli Lilly (LLY), whose triple-digit growth in Zepbound and Mounjaro enabled it to capture roughly 57% of U.S. GLP-1 prescriptions by late 2025.

- Seeking Alpha news

Novo Nordisk has also made some unforced errors, as its latest weight-loss drug, CagriSema, underperformed Eli Lilly's Zepbound (which uses the tirzepatide active ingredient) in clinical trials:

...people treated with CagriSema 2.4 mg/2.4 mg achieved a weight loss of 20.2% compared to 23.6% with tirzepatide at 84 weeks. The trial did not achieve its primary endpoint of demonstrating non-inferiority on weight loss for CagriSema compared to tirzepatide after 84 weeks.

- Novo Nordisk

However, there is a silver lining because a study published last week shows Novo Nordisk's GLP-1 active ingredient of semaglutide to be better at preserving patients' lean muscle mass compared to Eli Lilly's drugs:

peer review, were based on 1,800 patients on tirzepatide and 6,200 on semaglutide whose lean body mass [LBM] measurements were taken before and after they initiated the respective GLP-1 drugs.

According to the study, patients on tirzepatide indicated 1.1%, 1.5%, 1.3%, and 2.0% greater LBM loss at 3, 6, 9, and 12 months, respectively, compared to those on semaglutide...

- Analytics firm nference, Seeking Alpha

So as the market matures, I expect doctors and patients to opt for the drug that preserves muscle better, as that is linked to better overall health and longevity outcomes.

Pricing headwinds are leading to margin deterioration

Novo Nordisk has been posting larger misses in EBITDA margins vs. consensus estimates recently:

This is due to more intense competition and the US government's pricing interventions via the Most Favored Nations (MFN) pricing policy, which matches US drug prices to that of the lowest prices in peer nations.

I expect these headwinds to continue along with the additional headwind of patent expirations (discussed in the following section) to put further pressure on gross margins:

Patent expirations are another headwind to sales and profits

The key active ingredient, semaglutide, in Novo Nordisk's GLP-1 drugs Ozempic and Wegovy has lost patent expirations in some key markets such as Canada, Brazil, China, and India. This has opened the floodgates to low-cost generic alternatives. For example, in India, many Indian pharmaceutical companies are launching GLP-1 generics at $14 a month. For context, in the US, the cost is $199 to $349 a month.

Now, India makes up a small portion of NVO's overall sales currently. But the risk here is that patent expirations can cannibalize sales and profits in other large markets such as China (~5-6% of overall sales).

Note that both Ozempic and Wegovy have already seen a steady deceleration or even declining sales:

I expect to see further sales stagnancy and decline for these drugs over the medium term.

Undervaluation of NVO stock is an upside risk

NVO is trading at a 1-yr PE of 12.4x, which is 18% below the sectoral comps' median multiple of 15.2x:

This discount valuation for NVO vs. its peers has not been normal, as the stock has typically traded at a relative 5.6% premium over the last year:

Relative to its longer-term 1-year forward PEs as well, NVO's multiple now is at a 42% discount to the longer-term median of 21.4x.

Hence, I perceive some upside valuation risk to the stock.

I expect the downtrend to continue

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using technical analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

NVO Technical Analysis

The monthly chart of NVO stock shows an overall downtrend and a recent strong bearish engulfing candle. Due to this seller momentum, I expect more downside after a momentary pullback up.

Takeaway and Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bearish due to declining market shares along with broad-based sales and margin headwinds.
  • Valuation: Bullish, as NVO stock seems undervalued from multiple perspectives.
  • Technical Analysis: Bearish due to strong selling pressure.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bearish, Sell

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DXP Enterprises: Organic Growth Outpaces M&A, Margins Improve (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In February, after a weak Q3 report, I recommended tactically holding stocks and waiting for the release of the Q4 report, as there were concerns that the report might be weaker than expected. However, DXP Enterprises, Inc. (DXPE) was pleased with the advance of the consensus forecast for both revenue and EPS. Against the background of a good report, the stock has grown even more, and since my Buy recommendation in October last year, the total growth has been about 27%. The strategy chosen by the management to diversify the business and reduce dependence on oil and gas makes revenue more stable and more predictable. At the same time, due to the growth of financial indicators, the multiples have decreased slightly, and for many of them, the company's shares are now trading at a discount to the sectoral median values. Considering the financial situation and the current multiplier rating, I am upgrading the rating from Hold to Buy. According to my estimate, by building a DCF model, the fair value of the shares is $185 in the baseline scenario with an upside of 14.5% to the current price ($161.7 at the time of writing).

Strong Quarter Driven by Organic Growth and IPS Expansion

The latest key event in the company's business is related to the release of the Q4 financial statements and the market's reaction to the demonstrated results. I liked the results of the published report. The company managed to beat market expectations in terms of revenue and EPS. I would like to analyze key aspects of the reporting so that further forecasts for Q1 from the next chapter (the report is due out in May) are more understandable to the reader. But before that, I recommend that you read my first two articles about the company, where I have analyzed in detail all the company's activities, its business model, and its development strategy.

Revenue for the quarter increased by 12% yoy to $527.4 million. For comparison, I predicted about $500 million due to fewer working days and a seasonal slowdown in business by the end of the year, and the consensus forecast was $499 million, which is almost 6% lower than the actual result. Compared to Q3, revenue increased by 2.7%. An important caveat is that revenue from the core business grew strongly this quarter, and only part of the revenue was added by the absorbed companies. Organic revenue increased by as much as 16% to $505.5 million, and additional revenue from new assets even decreased by $13 million. This indicates a high demand for the company's solutions, as evidenced by the growth of sales per business day increased by 12% yoy to $8.5 million.

The situation has not changed by segment for several consecutive quarters—two key segments are growing at double-digit rates, while the third continues to stagnate. Revenue at Service Centers increased by 14.6% yoy to $356.2 million. In the 10-K, management points to high demand and the growth of large orders for adhesives in some regions, as well as growing interest in metalworking and air compressors. IPS revenue grew even faster, by 12.7%, to $110.0 million. IPS is more marginal, and high revenue growth rates help to increase the overall marginality of the business. According to management, the segment's revenue increased mainly due to new large client projects, as well as continued diversification into water and wastewater. But SCS sales have not grown, which has been attributed to lower activity from oil and gas customers for several quarters in a row. For investors, it is more important that the first two segments grow, and if IPS is growing faster than Service Centers, then this is doubly good, since the operating margin is growing along with it.

That's what happened in the end—operating profit increased by 18.8% yoy to $46.7 million. Profit growth faster than revenue resulted in an improvement in the operating margin from 8.3% to 8.9%. The adjusted EBITDA margin also increased to 11.2% from 10.7%. The IPS segment became the main engine of growth, where the operating margin increased from 15.6% to 18.0%. Operating expenses grew in Q4, but higher sales growth at Service Centers and IPS helped boost overall margins. The situation turned out to be slightly lower at the net profit level, but due to a one-time strong increase in the effective tax rate from 6% to 26%. As a result, the net margin decreased to 4.3% from 4.5%. Adjusted EPS rose from $1.29 to $1.39, with a consensus forecast of $1.30. I was close in my forecast, expecting $1.40 EPS. EPS growth was marginally impacted by share buybacks, but otherwise all the growth was due to last year's weak results and strong operating results in Q4’2026.

Overall, the company's report turned out to be strong, except for a nuance related to a strong increase in tax expenses. But even with this in mind, all indicators have grown at a faster pace, primarily due to organic business growth, not acquisitions. If DXP continues at the same pace, the stock will inevitably rise by another 10-20%.

Consensus and Management Aligned on Growth Outlook

Management does not make forecasts, either quarterly or yearly. From the management's comments, it can be concluded that they expect both organic growth in both business segments and acquisition-driven growth to continue. The situation with oil and gas is also expected to improve in the second half of the year. In the 10-K, the company writes that it expects growth rates similar to those in 2025, but subject to maintaining a favorable macroeconomic environment. Interest expenses are also expected to rise, as the company needs financing to maintain its growth rate. This will put pressure on net income, but it will reduce tax expenses.

The market consensus for the full year 2026 assumes revenue growth of 10.20% yoy to $2.22 billion and EPS of 12.92% yoy to $6.12. From the first quarter, the market expects revenue growth of 11.53% yoy to $531.5 million and EPS growth of 2.38% yoy to $1.29. Market expectations generally agree with management's comments. According to my expectations, the company will deliver a strong first quarter due to high demand in the two largest segments. I expect the following results for Q1: revenue of $532-535 million and EPS of $1.28-1.30.

Conservative DCF Still Shows Upside

Let's move on to the main thing, namely the assessment. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2026-2030, discounting the forecast FCFS, and then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030), and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap). With EV/EBITDA, this is the enterprise value from which net debt is also subtracted, which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

I have slightly revised the assumptions embedded in the DCF model towards greater conservatism. I forecast the revenue CAGR for 2026-2030 at 6% versus 12.6% for 2021-2025. Likewise, I expect that the revenue growth rate will gradually slow down with the saturation of the market and the gradual normalization of demand. The company will reach maturity by 2028-2029 and will no longer focus on growth but on maintaining the achieved market share and monetization. Besides, the company probably won't be able to grow revenue faster without new M&A deals. This will help increase the operating margin from 8.86% to 10.76% by 2030. Cost control will significantly affect the growth of the operating margin, but still, high competition will not allow it to grow faster. Capital expenditures will be kept in the range of 1.5-2% to maintain high business growth rates. Thus, the FCF yield for 2026-2030 will be about 6.20% versus 3.88% for 2021-2025. In fact, according to my forecast, from 2028 the company will focus not on quantity but on quality, trying to increase the marginality of the business and free cash flow.

I got the WACC for the company at 7.56% due to a beta of 0.95x, an equity risk premium (taken from Damodaran's table) of 4.3% for the USA, and a 4.34% risk-free rate, which I took as 10-year USA Government Bond Yields. The cost of debt was 6.5%. I use the latest TTM interest expense divided by the latest one-year quarterly average debt to get the simplified cost of debt.

Due to the growth of financial indicators, the multiples have decreased slightly. For many of them, the company's shares are currently trading at a discount to the sectoral medians. Stocks with parameters like those of DXP usually trade at large premiums to the market. I think the multiples will rise in the near future, especially if the company releases a strong Q1 report.

The DCF model unequivocally confirms my judgments. In the most negative scenario (with WACC rising and multiples falling), the fair value of the shares should be higher by 0-7% compared to the current market price. That is, there is no downside even in the worst-case scenario. In the basic scenario, the stock should be 11-15% more expensive, and in the positive scenario, it should be 22% more expensive. I upgrade the rating from Hold to Buy with a target price of $185 and an upside of 14.5%.

Moderate Buybacks with Focus on Balance Sheet

The debt burden on the business remains elevated due to frequent M&A transactions. By the end of 2025, the company's total debt amounted to $924.5 million, with cash in the accounts in the region of $303.8 million. This results in a net debt of $620.7 million. The debt/EBITDA ratio is only 4.13x, and the net debt/EBITDA is 2.74x. Operating profit exceeds interest expenses by almost 3 times. The company does not pay dividends, investing its free funds in business development and maintaining growth rates. DXP is holding buybacks, but in 2025 significantly fewer shares were bought back—only 0.2 million shares worth $17.0 million. Most likely, management is trying to buy back shares only on drawdowns.

Risks

The company's risks have not changed much since the last article. DXP is most dependent on a favorable macroeconomic environment, as recessions and stagnation in the economy lead to a reduction in orders and aggregate demand. DXP conducts a lot of M&A transactions to support growth, and this creates additional risks associated with the integration of purchased companies, overpaying for low-cost, and inflated expectations of synergy with new businesses. The other risks are standard, such as high competition, oil price volatility, and a high debt burden.

Final Thoughts

DXP is in a good financial position, and high demand allows for rapid growth in revenue and operating margins. High oil prices have also been playing into the hands of businesses since March. I expect the company to release a strong report for Q1, which will create additional interest in the stock, and its price will rise. That's why I'm upgrading the rating from Hold to Buy.
